title: Wall Street's Biggest AI Bull Just Got Louder
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives believes the AI boom is nowhere close to peaking, predicting the Nasdaq could surge to 30,000 within the next six to nine months as investor enthusiasm around AI chips, infrastructure and blockbuster tech IPOs keeps accelerating.

Is CBRS fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

Speaking in a CNBC interview after Cerebras Systems' (NASDAQ:CBRS) explosive market debut, Ives called the IPO a watershed moment for the tech sector and argued that demand for AI related investments remains exceptionally strong. Cerebras delivered one of the biggest IPO debuts in years, adding more than $4B in value on its first trading day and reinforcing the market's appetite for companies tied directly to the AI infrastructure race.

Ives described the current AI cycle as only being in the third inning of a much longer growth story. He pushed back aggressively against bearish calls on the tech sector, saying recent trips to Asia showed AI demand accelerating another 20% quarter over quarter. According to Ives, the real warning sign would be if hyperscalers and enterprises started pulling back AI spending, something he says is not happening at all right now.

The analyst also highlighted the enormous imbalance between supply and demand across the AI chip market, pointing specifically to Nvidia's (NVDA) reported 10 to 1 demand to supply ratio. He said the momentum should continue spreading across the semiconductor industry, including companies like AMD and even Intel as secondary beneficiaries of the broader AI buildout.

Beyond chips, Ives argued the next major phase of the AI trade could come from IPO markets. He described Cerebras as merely the appetizer, with future public offerings from companies like SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic potentially creating even more excitement around AI exposed assets.

Even OpenAI's recent controversies did not shake his confidence. Ives said the market's narrative around the company has become too negative compared to the underlying reality, arguing OpenAI will still become one of the foundational players of the AI era.

title: Stocks making the biggest moves midday: Cerebras, Microsoft, ServiceNow, Papa John's & more
content: Check out the companies making the biggest moves in midday trading: Microsoft — Shares rose nearly 4% after Pershing Square's Bill Ackman said he has been accumulating stock in the software company since its the stock fell in the wake of its second-quarter earnings report. The hedge fund manger disclosed the investment, but not its size, in a lengthy post on Friday ahead of his company's quarterly 13F filing. He argued its valuation was "highly compelling." ServiceNow — The AI platform's stock rose nearly 5% after it struck a multiyear partnership with Experian. The companies plan to work together on developing autonomous AI agents. Ford — Shares fell about 7% after the automaker's stock popped more than 20% in recent sessions as investors caught on to its battery storage ambitions tied to the broader AI trade . Analysts on Friday said recent gains appear driven more by enthusiasm around the planned business than to any near-term fundamentals. RBC analysts estimated the unit could eventually be worth $1 billion to $5 billion. Applied Materials — The semiconductor equipment supplier fell about 2% despite reporting fiscal second-quarter results that beat expectations on the top and bottom lines. Applied Materials earned $2.86 per share, excluding items, on revenue of $7.91 billion. Analysts surveyed by LSEG had expected per-share earnings of $2.66 on revenue of $7.65 billion. Chip stocks — A slew of semiconductor companies were lower as the iShares Semiconductor ETF was on pace to break a six-week win streak. Marvell Technology fell 3%, while Intel tumbled nearly 7%. ASML and Arm were down about 5% and 8%, respectively. Advanced Micro Devices shed 4%. Cerebras Systems — The chipmaker fell 5% after surging 68% Thursday in its Nasdaq debut. Starbucks — Shares were up less than 1% after announcing plans to lay off about 300 U.S. corporate employees in its third round of cuts since CEO Brian Niccol took over the company. Figma — Shares surged nearly 9% after the collaborative interface design tool posted first quarter earnings of 10 cents per share, on an adjusted basis, on revenues of $333 million. Analysts polled by LSEG had expected per-share earnings of 6 cents on revenues of $313 million. Magnum Ice Cream — The ice cream and frozen desert manufacturer jumped more than 17% after Reuters reported Blackstone and CD & R are some of the firms considering a bid for the company. Potential bidders are waiting to see summer sales before making any moves, Reuters reported. DexCom — Shares of the diabetes management company jumped almost 8% after DexCom said it's agreed with Elliott Investment Management to identify two new independent directors to join the board. Papa John's International — The stock jumped more than 4% after Reuters reported the company's largest franchisee is looking to buy the company. Irth Capital is working with the franchisee, who owns 10% of the company's domestic establishments, to take the company private. Gemini Space Station – The Winklevoss-owned crypto exchange surged 10% after reporting a $100 million strategic investment from Winklevoss Capital Fund. The company also topped revenue expectations for the first quarter and posted a narrower-than-expected loss than analysts anticipated, according to FactSet. Freeport McMoRan — The miner fell almost 5% as metals sold off across the board in Friday trading. Precious metals gold and silver fell around 3% and 9%, respectively. Industrial metal copper tumbled nearly 5%. — CNBC's Nick Wells, Sarah Min and Tanaya Macheel contributed reporting

title: AI IPO boom and chip demand could lift Nasdaq to 30K, Ives says—CNBC interview
content:
[Artificial intelligence circuit board 5]
Jonathan Kitchen

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives predicts the Nasdaq will reach 30,000 within the next six to nine months, driven by big tech and the expanding AI chip sector. 

Ives made the bold forecast following Cerebras’s blockbuster IPO, which saw gains exceeding $4 billion—the best single-day IPO performance since 2000.

In an interview with Bloomberg Television, Ives described the Cerebras (CBRS [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CBRS]) IPO as “a watershed moment for the tech sector,” noting that investor appetite for AI-exposed companies remains exceptionally high. He characterized the current AI boom as still being in its early stages, saying the market is in “the third inning” of a “nine-inning baseball game.”

Ives dismissed bearish perspectives on the tech sector, pointing to accelerating demand he witnessed during a recent trip to Asia. “When I was in Asia just a few weeks ago, demand the spots accelerated 20% quarter over quarter,” he explained. The analyst said he would only become concerned if companies began slowing their pace of AI-related spending, which he has not observed.

The chip trade will continue to climb due to overwhelming demand relative to supply, according to Ives, who noted that Nvidia's (NVDA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/NVDA]) demand-to-supply ratio stands at 10 to one. He expects AMD shares to rise at least another 20% and sees positive momentum spreading to Intel and other semiconductor companies as second, third, and fourth derivative plays on the AI revolution.

Regarding OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]), which has faced negative headlines, including a reported breakdown in its relationship with Apple (AAPL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AAPL]), Ives remained optimistic. “The narrative is a bit negative, but I think that’s actually much different than the reality,” he said, adding that OpenAI will be “one of the foundations of the AI revolution” despite near-term challenges.

Ives characterized the Cerebras offering as “the appetizer” to what’s coming, with highly anticipated IPOs from SpaceX (SPACE [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPACE]), OpenAI, and Anthropic still on the horizon. While acknowledging that Anthropic currently has “the best model,” he cautioned against judging companies based solely on recent news, emphasizing that the AI revolution is just beginning.

title: Cerebras IPO in focus as Nvidia earnings loom
content:
Epistrophy Capital Research Chief Market Strategist and The Drill Down Podcast host Cory Johnson joins Market Catalysts to discuss the Cerebras IPO and key factors to watch in Nvidia’s (NVDA) upcoming earnings report.

Video Transcript

00:00 Julie

Cerebras, such an interesting story. I talked to the CEO Andrew Feldman yesterday and we talked about, you know, the

00:08 Corey

Now he's a billionaire. He was happy.

00:09 Julie

He's worth yes, he said it's a pretty good day. A $3.2 billion dollars is reportedly how much he was worth after yesterday, um making these big chips that their argument is are better suited to AI, can do can uh reduce latency, make things work faster. Um, but I I guess I'm curious first of all, what do you think of Cerus specifically and then I'm curious what we think of like what it shows about the bigger environment.

00:46 Corey

So, I've been on this theme for a little while in the chip world that Nvidia's and I emailed you about this idea, but the Nvidia's obscene gross margins has made a lot of money for Nvidia. Nvidia, I saw this headline yesterday that Nvidia is now worth more than silver. The second most valuable thing in the world. Gold is more valuable than what

01:14 Julie

It's down a little bit today, but, you know.

01:16 Corey

Yeah. Nvidia. but uh down 4% maybe on after some China news didn't turn out to be so good, I think. Okay. So, uh, but Nvidia's margins are crazy high. I mean, they were high when they were 60% and now they're closer to 75%. And while that has allowed the company to make boat loads of money when everyone else in AI is bleeding money or a lot of companies and I are bleeding money. Um, it's left room for other solutions. So it's let other companies develop training chips uh like Tranium the and the TP from from Amazon TPUs at Google. Um AMD's

02:01 Julie

Yeah. So, it's

02:03 Corey

doing some business uh with Oracle and with others. Um, and and of course uh Intel's out there. But, um with with with its own offerings. But what's going to be interesting is what happens in inference. Because you might not need the chips that you needed for the Blackwell might have been great for inference. It was cheaper and everyone would have just stuck with that. But it's so expensive that lots of other companies have found room to be creative. Cerus one of them, Potronic company I'm invested in as well.

02:26 Julie

But Cerus's chips are also expensive, if not more expensive.

02:30 Corey

Yeah, but they do so much more and they do so much more on an inference level. So, they can manage so much more information so quickly. and it's a it's a brilliant design where they've got the memory kind of built into the chip. They've managed power and heat in ways that weren't thought to be possible. And they're just crazy honking and big. Did you guys talk about that much yesterday?

02:51 Julie

Yes, he held one up while we were talking, but we did talk about dinner plate or iPad or whoever it was. Or or as our our Dan Hally likened it to a pizza pie. Like you've got most of the chip makers that just have a slice or two, whereas

03:10 Corey

Well, even even the Blackwell's huge. I mean, you know

03:13 Julie

It is because they put different chips together though. They don't make it as a single chip to begin with.

03:16 Corey

Right. You buy the you buy the Chiplet. what they call. But but in fact it is a very large chip. Uh comes out of the die even bigger just the basic chip in itself and many layers. But um, you know, it's an interest I I I pitched this magazine story once.

03:34 Julie

Yes.

03:35 Corey

Recently to a friend who runs a

03:36 Julie

Which was what?

03:37 Corey

Which magazine? No, what was story big getting. I thought it'd be clever magazine story to show what the Intel chips, the CPUs looked used to look like and now the Nvidia chips. Now the Cerus thing and then they could fold out a big page. Nobody nobody listens to me, Julie. Except for you. Um, I think that uh, uh, what we're see from Cerus is that Nvidia left room in the market for different solutions for inference. When Nvidia came out of the gate about three or four years ago, suddenly dominating AI, putting up prints that were doubling revenues year after year. Other companies said, where are we going to be five years from now? Let's make chips for them. And this's what Cerus is, that's what Potron is. That's what some of the Intel chips are going to be. I could see AMD focusing on this as well. And so we'll see what the dominant chip company is in five years. You might not be Nvidia anymore.

04:36 Julie

Well, the the other question I have too is and I keep asking everybody this today. Right now, we are in a moment where no one cares how much this costs, meaning the

04:52 Corey

Absolutely true.

04:52 Julie

When do we get to that point of more price sensitivity where this like the the sort of like fever dream of compute at any cost starts to fade.

05:07 Corey

When there is capacity. and there isn't capacity. I don't know when that's going be. And it's no one knows when that's going to be and I've I spent uh the day yesterday at Oracle in Nashville, Oracle's headquarters in Nashville now. I spent the day with a bunch of executives there in an off the record briefing, but I learned a ton. And one of the things I can tell you and I hear this from every company I talk to at every stage of AI is they cannot build it fast enough, literally, they cannot build it fast enough. For every single thing they can build, they've already got a sale ready and they can sell more if they can build more. And so the whole the race is to build it fast. And so that's what you see. And and this is different than the cycles we used to see. The cycles we used to see in compute used to be governed by um the the evolving pace that used to seem really fast of a new Intel chip and a new Windows operating system. And that is out the window. This is about building for a future of AI that we don't even know what it is. Agenic AI is driving growth at rates that were seeming impossible just two years ago when AI growth rates were jaw dropping. And we don't even know where this is going to go.

06:21 Julie

What's so fascinating to me about this stage, if as you frame it like that is, you see anecdotal evidence out there that the data centers that are out there are not running at full capacity.

06:40 Julie

At least because it seems like everybody is building for the eventual, right? But that right now, it's not necessarily being used.

06:51 Corey

Yeah, I I think the use cases and how data centers work is evolving. So let me explain what I mean by that. Some use cases are very GPU intensive and don't really need a ton of memory at a given moment. Others might need a lot of memory at a certain moment when it's doing a lot of of of uh uh big compute problems and not really using the GPU as much. And so you build a um a data center for max capacity. But you don't run it at the max all the time. You don't run it at max power, you don't run max systems. And we see changes uh ongoing literally right now. There are designs of how to use data centers in ways that can shift loads within the data center to use the things that the user needs, the business needs at the moments that it needs them and free up other resources otherwise. But the but building for max capacity and not running at max capacity, you know, when you're when you're driving an F1 car, you're not at full tilt in the turns. And so the data center's not being used to capacity. That's a big part of that.

08:14 Julie

Um, what were your other takeaways from that uh Oracle visit?

08:18 Corey

Oh, I have so many. Uh, I've got so much to write about that. Um, that this isn't slowing down. And that that the growth rates coming out of Oracle, I'm biased on this one. I I own the stock. I like the people at Oracle. Um which not never not everybody likes people at Oracle. I love the people at Oracle. Um, they are growing at breakneck paces and they have been a breakneck pace. They've been growing faster than anyone else in the data center business for a couple years in a row. But they've they've got this real advantage that you wouldn't think is an advantage. They came into this a little bit later than some of their competitors. And so they don't have the technical debt. You know, Amazon has to support every single possible use that they've ever supported in the past. So they have hundreds of different products they have to support at AWS. Oracle doesn't have that problem. They've got the best and the brightest and the newest stuff. And so they can run things faster and cheaper and they're winning business in that regard. It's interesting to see. My Amazon sales people tell me, I was texting with an Amazon sales person just this morning. and he's like, oh, let me get this straight. So we they have less to offer and customers want that. But I think when you tell customers what you can give them and you can give them compute. We see Oracle making great sales there and doing a real big business.

09:47 Julie

Okay, but all the other stuff that Amazon is supporting, they're making money from all that stuff too.

09:51 Corey

Everybody's making money on this. Yeah.

09:53 Julie

So, I mean, so that's that's not a terrible.

09:55 Corey

It's a good business to be in when you can charge whatever you want.

09:56 Julie

couple more things because we have a lot to get through with you. Um, I do want to come back around to Nvidia and what you're going to be watching next week in those numbers, especially given the run that Nvidia has been on again, right? It was so interesting this year to see Nvidia sort of lagging everything else in semiconductors, right? And then like rocketing up and the stock.

10:20 Corey

Not the business.

10:20 Julie

Not the business. Yeah. The business has been doing what it's been doing, which is well. Um, but, you know, coming back, rocketing back to a new record high. It's pulling back a little bit today as we talked about. But like, what are you going to be watching for to see if it continues to support that enthusiasm in the market?

10:40 Corey

I want to hear things about how they're incorporating Gro and incorporating new technologies. I want to hear about the use of inference. Inference is going to be so much bigger than training, something that I didn't really understand a couple years ago, but we're starting to see that uh already take place. Um, I want to hear about use cases because um they're just fascinating and interesting and and Jensen such a showman when he gets on that conference call, that we're going to learn a lot about what's happening in this industry. But you know, Nvidia is sold out. And so we're going to we're going to hear what's happening with sales going forward. You know, the quarterly earnings is is the difference between the business and the stock. It's the moment when we can really hear what's happening at the business. Um and so we love that.

11:27 Julie

Are there any risks for Nvidia at this point?

11:30 Corey

Uh there's all kinds of execution risks, there's development risks, there's plans for what comes next. We don't really expect that in this conference call, but they've got to keep iterating and keep inventing. They still control uh uh the future of AI. They're still everyone's first choice. If you can have whatever you want in a data center, you want Nvidia chips.

11:51 Julie

I mean, I guess the risk to your point about on execution is still like that they can't make enough that they, you know, that there's some sort of supply chain. Well, and the

11:58 Corey

this the holdup right now, the bottleneck is memory. and it continues to be. And so we're going to listen to what the the memory bottleneck means for Nvidia. I'm also listening for changes in power. So the way power gets to the actual CPU, Nvidia has said that they're going to change that dramatically in the next couple uh years, even this year. And we haven't heard details about that. They put out a blog post that listed 18 companies that they might be working with. Yeah. One of those companies started putting up press releases right away because they were running out of money, losing boatloads of money. That stock uh the stock that I'm short um has has gone straight up into my face. What's that? I'll tell you. It's called Navitas. They've got tiny, horrible revenues. It's a money losing business. but on the basis of press releases in hope, the stock has rocketed. And um, we'll listen for what those changes in power will be and we'll listen perhaps for what uh partners they might engage with in that. They haven't picked anybody yet. They certainly haven't picked Navitas, but uh some people are guessing that's a case or maybe retail investors.

13:20 Julie

Well, we had geothermal company go public this week also on the on the power front. That's power.

13:26 Corey

IPOs are back.

13:28 Julie

Furo. Speaking of IPOs, let's end here. So there is we've been waiting for the SpaceX S1. I know you in particular have been waiting for this. SpaceX, it's really hard to say SpaceX S1. In any case, it might be coming next week. Um, what are like the three things you are going to be looking for in that S1?

13:58 Corey

So, there's among the things I'm going to be looking for is um how big is the SpaceX business, the rocket business, and and what how much of that growth can be seen outside of the US government? Because an IPO is all about what could happen in the future, not just what has happened in the past. Even though you get this incredible glimpse at what is happening right now and in the past. So how big is the rocket business and where is the growth going to come from when when it's not the US government? Another thing I'm looking for is the thud. The thud. How big is this thing going to be? Uh one of my uh colleagues thinks it's going to be 3,000 pages.

14:42 Julie

Oh, the physical physical red herring.

14:45 Corey

Because I've got to read the whole thing.

14:46 Julie

Wait, are you are you actually you're not printing it out, though?

14:49 Corey

I have never I guess I'm printing it out. I haven't thought about that. I have never worked on an IPO without actually printing out the S1. There's something about the big dropping. I I do more and more on the iPad taking notes but still the thought, how big is this thing going to be? This is a really complex business. Um, the the hot I'll tell you right now, the hot conversation we're going to say, it's not really a SpaceX IPO, it's it's really a Starlink IPO because Starlink is the big business here and probably the profitable business. Not SpaceX. So, we'll look for how what the the third thing I'm going to look for is the churn with the third thing. I'm looking for a thousand things. The churn rate's going to be really important. How good is the Starlink business? Where can it grow to? You know, internet, not having to be tethered down is a great idea. It's great when you're on a plane, it's great when you're in a rural environment. How big is that business? You know, they're offering things, selling things on United Airlines, uh partnership with T-Mobile. It's internet, it's not really voice. So what could that business be and what does it look like? And what is the churn rate? Will they give us those numbers? Those are some of the things I'm looking at. Um I'm doing a ton of work on this deal. I've already written 40 pages of my preview and I haven't even seen the S1 yet. So I'm getting ready. Amazing. Um but where my clients are demanding IPO coverage and we're going to give it.

16:30 Julie

I am still impressed that you are using your own brain and your own eyes to read through a hard copy of this S1 versus feeding it into one of the LLMs and like getting them to pull out this thing these things you're looking for. How many pages? How much you say? 3,000 pages you think?

16:49 Corey

That's my it's my partner's guessing 3,000 pages. We'll see, it's going to be big. But that that to that point, it is such a any one of these at Twitter, right, X, a very complex business with all sorts of nuances. That's just a tiny part of this thing that is going to be rocket launching, social media, chip manufacturing, Terrafab's going to be in here somewhere. uh XAI. How big is XAI? Is it bigger than uh uh anthropic? Probably not, but maybe. We're going to take a look at that.

17:34 Julie

Um, I wonder if there's anything like on Polymarketer or about how many pages the S1 will be.

17:40 Corey

There might be by the end of this hit.

17:42 Julie

But I bet that somebody knows and so then you're going to get somebody who's who, you know, there's definitely

17:48 Corey

At least in this case they're regulated. information on that. Exactly. There's someone at the at RR Donley the printer is going to be like, just paginate a little bit different. I got a bet on this.

17:58 Julie

Corey, it's great to see you.

18:00 Corey

Good to see you too.

18:00 Julie

I will definitely be pinging you once that comes out.

18:02 Corey

Great, I'll be here or there or somewhere.

18:04 Julie

Somewhere, we'll talk about.
